own_review.issue: Fallback failures are caught as 'catch (Exception ignored)' and discarded entirely, unlike CredentialHelper and ProcessRunner which addSuppressed; the final RuntimeException carries neither cause nor suppressed exceptions, losing diagnostics and being inconsistent across the three sites.
An InterruptedException thrown by a fallback check (process.waitFor) is swallowed in the ignored-catch without restoring the interrupt flag.

The startsWith(binDirectory) guards are unnecessary: pre-PR the guards in ProcessRunner/DisabledIf existed solely to stop the recursive retry (startProcess/check calling itself), and the PR removes the recursion in favor of direct start() calls; command/helper names never contain '/'. In CredentialHelper the guard is entirely new behavior unrelated to adding the homebrew fallback. Matches the maintainer's view: revert.

The loop relies on processBuilder.command(command) mutating shared builder state between iterations; it is only correct because 'executable' is captured before the loop and set(0, ...) fully overwrites element 0. Fragile pattern; a fresh ProcessBuilder per attempt (as in the other two files) would be safer.

No recursion exists in the new code: localProcessBuilder.start() is called directly; the recursive startProcess(localCommand) call exists only in the pre-PR code (verified at base commit). Any claim of recursion in the PR is a fabrication.
path.contains(binDirectory) is a loose substring match; pre-existing pattern, not introduced by this PR.

points.point: CredentialHelper loop mutates processBuilder.command in-place but stays correct because executable is captured pre-loop; suggest fresh ProcessBuilder per attempt for consistency with the other two files
points.note: Verified accurate mutation analysis; matches my own finding.

points.point: Test input-shape inconsistency: production always passes bare 'docker-credential-' names (DockerRegistryConfigAuthentication ctor verified) while other tests use geExecutableAbsolutePath, which the startsWith guard does not cover
points.note: All factual claims verified in repo, including the typo'd method name geExecutableAbsolutePath; engages the same guard the human flagged.

points.point: ProcessRunnerTests has no tests for the macOS fallback (neither /usr/local/bin nor /opt/homebrew/bin); runWhenProcessDoesNotStart does not assert suppressed exceptions
points.note: Verified: no fallback/suppressed coverage in ProcessRunnerTests.

points.point: DisabledIfProcessUnavailableTests is a single smoke test that does not exercise the fallback logic
points.note: Verified true, but unit-testing a JUnit condition's PATH fallback is impractical; soft coverage nit.

points.point: Trailing-slash constant inconsistency between CredentialHelper and the other two classes

missed: Human: DisabledIfProcessUnavailableCondition startsWith guard is unrelated/unnecessary, should be reverted
Human: ProcessRunner startsWith guard is unrelated/unnecessary, should be reverted

Own: DisabledIfProcessUnavailableCondition discards fallback exceptions via catch(Exception ignored) without addSuppressed; final RuntimeException has no cause (inconsistent with the other two files, loses diagnostics)
exceeds: Verified cross-file evidence (DockerRegistryConfigAuthentication) for production input shape

points.point: DisabledIfProcessUnavailableTests is a minimal smoke test; suggest adding a test verifying both fallback candidates, self-labeled low priority
points.note: Verified true but impractical soft coverage nit; same grade as the equivalent v5 point.

points.point: Trailing-slash convention inconsistency across the three files, explicitly 'no action needed'
points.note: Accurate, pre-existing style, no action proposed.

points.point: Claims the ProcessRunner startsWith guard 'correctly prevents the original bug where recursive fallback could create double-prefixed paths like /usr/local/bin/usr/local/bin/docker'
points.note: Fabricated: there was no such original bug (the pre-PR recursion carried the same guard), and the new code has no recursion at all, so the guard is dead weight. v7 endorses the exact guard the maintainer wants reverted, while invoking a recursion that exists only pre-PR (the known trap). Self-contradictory with its own 'eliminates this concern entirely' sentence.

points.point: Flags catch(Exception ignored) in DisabledIfProcessUnavailableCondition but declares it 'acceptable' because 'the approach matches the pattern in ProcessRunner'
points.note: Right line, wrong conclusion on a real issue, justified by a false claim: ProcessRunner addSuppressed-es fallback failures into the rethrown exception, whereas DisabledIf discards them entirely and its RuntimeException carries no cause/suppressed. The patterns do not match.

missed: Human: DisabledIfProcessUnavailableCondition startsWith guard is unrelated/unnecessary, should be reverted
Human: CredentialHelper startsWith guard is unrelated/unnecessary, should be reverted

qualitative: The maintainer's only real objection is that the new startsWith(binDirectory) guards are unrelated scope creep to revert across all three files; neither candidate reached that conclusion, and v7 argued the opposite, praising the ProcessRunner guard as a fix for a fabricated 'original double-prefix bug' tied to a recursion that exists only in pre-PR code. v7 also found the genuinely problematic catch(Exception ignored) in DisabledIfProcessUnavailableCondition but waved it off with the false claim that it matches ProcessRunner's pattern, when ProcessRunner preserves failures via addSuppressed and DisabledIf discards them. v5 was fully grounded: every factual claim verified against the repo (including the typo'd geExecutableAbsolutePath and the DockerRegistryConfigAuthentication input-shape evidence) and it surfaced two real coverage gaps, though it too missed the revert-the-guards objection and the exception-swallowing inconsistency. Neither candidate made false absence claims. v5 clearly outperforms v7 here on factual reliability and substance.
